file_name: Quaker Chemical Corporation - NON COMPETITION AND NON SOLICITATION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: 5. Injunctive Relief; Attorneys Fees. Each Seller agrees that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the damage to Buyer will be inestimable and that therefore any remedy at Law or in monetary damages shall be inadequate.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Injunctive Relief; Attorneys Fees. Each Seller agrees that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the damage to Buyer will be inestimable and that therefore any remedy at Law or in monetary damages may be inadequate. However, Buyer shall make all possible efforts to quantify the damages before seeking injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: The inserted sentence 'However, Buyer shall make all possible efforts to quantify the damages before seeking injunctive relief' creates an in-text contradiction. The initial statement suggests monetary damages are inherently inadequate due to the inestimable nature of the harm. The added sentence implies that Buyer should attempt to quantify the damages, suggesting monetary relief *could* be adequate if damages can be estimated, thus contradicting the claim of inherent inadequacy. This change causes uncertainity in the enforcement on whether an injunction relief is the correct approach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Injunctive Relief; Attorneys Fees. Each Seller agrees that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the damage to Buyer will be inestimable and that therefore any remedy at Law or in monetary damages may be inadequate. However, Buyer shall make all possible efforts to quantify the damages before seeking injunctive relief. Accordingly, the parties agree that Buyer shall, in addition to monetary damages incurred by reason of any such breach or potential breach, without the necessity of posting any bond or similar instrument (and Sellers hereby irrevocably waive any right it may have to require the obtaining, furnishing or posting of any such bond or similar instrument) be entitled to seek injunctive relief (including specific performance) against the Sellers for breach of this Agreement. If any action or proceeding shall be commenced to enforce this Agreement or any right arising in connection with this Agreement, the prevailing party in such action or proceeding shall be entitled to recover from the other party the reasonable attorneys' fees, costs and expenses incurred by such prevailing party in connection with such action or proceeding.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that monetary damages are inadequate. The changed text introduces a requirement for the Buyer to attempt to quantify damages before seeking injunctive relief, which implies that monetary damages could potentially be adequate, thus contradicting the initial statement and the contradicted text which includes the same clause. This introduces uncertainty about when injunctive relief is appropriate, creating a direct conflict.
